entry.title: The Architecture Question Your AI Strategy Can't Avoid
entry.content:
IDEA Forum - Copenhagen, 2025

The most common architectural response to an AI readiness problem is the wrong one: consolidate everything into a single system, run AI on top of it, declare the foundation solved.

It doesn't work.

Large organizations don't have a consolidation problem. They have a connection problem. A customer exists differently in three systems. A product code means one thing in finance and something adjacent in supply chain. That fragmentation is structural,  built over years of acquisitions and org changes. Centralizing it would take years and introduce a single point of failure.

The right answer isn't to move the data. It's to connect it.

---

## What the framework points to

The ODI's Framework for AI-Ready Enterprise Data,  publishing this month through the IDEA programme, defines 21 concrete criteria across four pillars. Read across them, and a pattern emerges. The requirements don't point toward centralization. They point toward connection: connecting distributed data without moving it, enriching it with semantic context, governing it in a way that holds for autonomous agents as well as humans.

That pattern has a name: a data fabric.

A data fabric is not a product, there's no software you install and declare yourself AI-ready. It's an architectural approach: integration pipelines, active catalogues, and governance engines working together to make distributed data usable. It leaves the data where it is and creates a unified virtual access layer on top - harmonizing contents at the point of query, surfacing a verified, governed response to whoever or whatever is asking.

The ODI's supplementary research on data fabrics, released alongside the main framework, maps this to three specific capabilities: integration, cataloguing, and governance.

---

## Why it matters for SAP organizations specifically

Each of those three capabilities has a direct read-across to the SAP data landscape.

Integration is where entity resolution lives, continuous matching across connected systems, merging fragmented records into the golden records AI can actually trust. SAP Business Data Cloud operationalizes this for the SAP ecosystem, harmonizing data objects across S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba, and the broader application landscape into governed, consistent business entities.

Cataloguing is where most organizations are further behind than they think. A static data catalog is a directory, useful for humans, insufficient for AI. What the framework calls for is active cataloguing: machine learning continuously profiling data as it arrives, tracking lineage, building a semantic map that AI agents can traverse to understand not just what data exists, but how everything relates.

Governance is the capability that scales worst without intentional architecture. Traditional governance was built for humans who read policies and exercise judgment. AI agents don't do that. The framework's answer is policy-as-code: access rules enforced at the infrastructure level, before the model ever sees the data.

---

## The timing

The framework publishes this month. On June 25th, the ODI team (Elena Simperl, Paul Groth, and Stuart Coleman) will present it live and take questions. One hour, free.

[Register for the June 25th webinar]

And if you want early access to the full report before it's publicly indexed:

[Join IDEA]

---

The organizations that figure out the connection problem are the ones that will scale AI reliably. Everyone else will keep running pilots.

Where does your organization sit? Have you started the architectural conversation, or is it still downstream of the model selection debate?

---

IDEA — the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI — is a programme of independent research and peer learning developed in partnership between SAP and the Open Data Institute. Nine roundtables. 140+ senior executives. Three research briefs. And a framework that changes how enterprise AI readiness gets measured.

entry.title: Change audit for workflows?
entry.content:
Hi SF Community,

Is anyone aware of change audit reporting on SuccessFactors workflows?   There does not seem to be anything built into the system - the only information available in the UI is the date/time of the most recent change, and the name of the person who made the change.

There's no information on what changed at that time, or any history of previous changes.

Lacking any system audit, has anyone found a manual method that satisfies your internal and external audit team?

Thanks in advance for any info you can share!

Ricardo Rojas

entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud - Story Filter, Page Filter, Chart Filter, Table Filter, .....(all about fil
entry.content:
Hi @IngoH ,

I’ve created a Position-based Story Report and am exploring options to filter the “Vacant” field using an Input Control.

Since Vacant is a Boolean field, it returns values as True/False. However, I’ve noticed that it is categorised under Measures rather than Dimensions, which prevents it from being directly used in an input control.

I attempted a workaround by creating a calculated column to convert the Boolean value into a string (e.g., “Vacant” / “Not Vacant”), but this approach didn’t work as expected for filtering purposes.

My goal is to apply a filter so that the report displays only records where:

Vacant = True

Has anyone encountered this limitation or found an effective way to enable filtering on Boolean fields in Story Reports—particularly through input controls or alternative design approaches?

Any guidance or best practices would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks in advance,

Harini

entry.title: Re: Why the idoc hierarchy are different in KAP & K4Q system. How to correct them?
entry.content:
Who is sending IDOC?  You can debug Idoc using BD87 -> reprocessin debug.

This looks like logic to control segment under one parent is broken. If ECC is building the logic, then check 

WE41 -> Process code -> Display That FM must be having logic to create new parent if segment max is reached. Debug this FM.

entry.title: SAP Xperience CWB 2026: Conexão, Aprendizado Prático e a Força da Nossa Comunidade!
entry.content:
Olá, comunidade SAP! 💙💛

O SAP Xperience CWB 2026 foi um verdadeiro sucesso e deixou uma marca incrível em nossa comunidade local. Foi uma manhã intensa de muito aprendizado, troca de experiências, conexões habilidades e, acima de tudo, muita colaboração dentro do ecossistema SAP.



 

Esta foto oficial representa muito mais do que um encontro: ela reflete a energia de pessoas dispostas a aprender, compartilhar conhecimento e crescer juntas. Agradecemos sinceramente a cada participante pela dedicação, presença e pela atmosfera inspirada que criamos juntos!

 



 

🚀Teoria e Prática Caminhando Juntas

Sabemos que a teoria é importante, mas é na prática que o verdadeiro aprendizado acontece. Durante o evento, os participantes literalmente colocaram a "mão na massa"! Eles exploraram o sistema, trocaram insights em tempo real e vivenciaram o universo SAP de forma prática, interativa e colaborativa. Foi incrível acompanhar o engajamento, a evolução e o brilho nos olhos de cada um que esteve presente no laboratório.

 



🧠 Dedicação que Inspira: Nossos Instrutores

Ver o conhecimento sendo transmitido de forma tão generosa é o que nos dá orgulho de fazer parte da SAP Community Curitiba. Por isso, deixamos o nosso muito obrigado a Luiz Carlos de Souza e Jhean Lanski . Eles dedicaram voluntariamente seu tempo, energia e expertise técnica para guiar os participantes ao longo de toda uma atividade hands-on, impulsionando uma jornada de novos talentos e inspirando a todos nós.

 



 

🎬O Time de Bastidores: Quem Fez Acontecer

Por trás de um grande evento, há sempre um tempo incrível trabalhando com paixão. Queremos expressar nossa profunda gratidão às voluntárias que cuidaram de cada detalhe com tanto carinho e dedicação:

  • Simone Azevedo , que liderou com maestria todo o planejamento e a organização do evento.

  • Beatriz Sakai e Bruna Gritti Brizola , que no dia do evento comandaram os bastidores, garantindo que tudo corresse perfeitamente na Escola Politécnica da PUCPR.

     

Obrigado por doarem seu tempo e talento. Vocês fizeram toda a diferença!

💙Nossa Gratidão aos Apoiadores

Iniciativas como essas só saem do papel quando encontramos parceiros que acreditam no poder da comunidade. Nossos agradecimentos especiais às empresas e projetos que nos promoveram e caminharam conosco nessa jornada:

  • CHpro Solutions

  • Escola Politécnica PUCPR

  • Lab2Learn | SAP BTP

  • Moovi

  • Abap para meninas

Vocês são parte fundamental do ecossistema e do fortalecimento da nossa comunidade local. Obrigado por acreditarem neste propósito!

Nos vemos nos próximos eventos! 🚀💙💛

🎯Organização: Simone Azevedo | Luiz Carlos de Souza | Adriano Marques de Oliveira

#ComunidadeSAP #ComunidadeSAPCWB #SAPXperienceCWB #SAPXPCWB

entry.title: Re: WFA Empty Screen After OLAP Success – Fact Table Jobs...
entry.content:
Hi @ricardosuarez 

The default of the landing page was set to a blank page.  The reason is that if someone has RBP issue or customer support, they can still go to Workforce Analytics and can go to the Admin in Workforce Analytics.  If the landing page is setup with KPI dashboard, user with RBP issue or customer support won't be able to go in at all.

Since you are expecting to see something, I've changed the default setting to KPI dashboard.  Please check. 

Also, do you still have issue not able to see any measures on the left-hand side menu?  If you still have this issue, please create support ticket, they should be able to help with functional issue.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: SAP4ME change IT contact email and Name
entry.content:
As per your description, I understand you would like to change IT contact person for SAP Ariba Suite.

However, our support team does not have the authority to directly assist the customer in updating the IT contact person. The IT contact person is usually designated by the customer based on the provided information when signing the relevant cloud product contract. If the customer needs to change the IT contact person, they need to contact their Account Manager according to the KBA 2991636 - How to change the Main IT Contact/Technical Administrator for an SAP BTP Global Account to process this, which is also done with the consideration of protecting the customer's data security.

I have found the Account Manager contact information for you as follows. Please contact him for further processing.

entry.title: PFCG Role Generation After S/4HANA Upgrade: New T-Code Only or Full Authorization Reload?
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

I am currently working on an SAP S/4HANA upgrade project from S/4HANA 2021 to S/4HANA 2023.

I have a question regarding role maintenance and authorization generation after the upgrade.

Assume that a role already contains multiple transactions (T-codes) and has been generated successfully. After the upgrade, if I add a new T-code to the existing role and then perform authorization maintenance/generation:

Will SAP only load the authorization objects related to the newly added T-code?
Or will SAP reload all authorization objects associated with every T-code contained in the role?

Any explanation regarding the role generation mechanism in PFCG would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

QuocDT5

entry.title: Re: The issue of program RK_CYCLE_UPLOAD_DOWNLOAD
entry.content:
Dear Liangnan,

Thank you for the information.

I have checked the available SAP documentation. In allocation cycle maintenance, the receiver rule “Variable Portions” is supported for receiver tracing factors. SAP documentation also explains that, when this receiver rule is used, the system determines the allocation base based on the maintained variable portion method.

However, for transaction KSEUD / Excel upload-download, the upload is handled through a predefined template and specific upload logic. Based on the behavior you reported, it appears that the tracing factor “Variable Investments” may not be accepted by the upload program, even though it may be possible to maintain it manually in the cycle.

Unfortunately, I could not see the attachment from my side. Therefore, I am not able to fully verify the exact error message or the uploaded template.

If you need further assistance, I would recommend opening a case with SAP Support and attaching the relevant screenshots, upload file, cycle details, and exact error message. We will then look into the issue further from the system/program side.

Thank you for your understanding.

Best regards,
Nghia

entry.title: BRIM Partner Settlement vs PS-CD Revenue Distribution: How Close Can It Really Get?
entry.content:
1. Introduction

When a project sits in the public sector, SAP PS-CD is often assumed to be in scope. I would pause here.

Some government organisations run simply as high-volume money-in / money-out shared-services platforms. No Funds Management, no statutory billing requirements. In these cases, a lean FI-CA and Convergent Invoicing (CI) implementation is often enough.

The real fork usually comes with the next requirement, and it is a common one in the public sector. A portion, or all of the collected cash needs to be forwarded to a third party. If you have worked with SAP PS-CD, you will know the Revenue Distribution capability. Public sector organisations use it to distribute collected amounts on behalf of third parties.

Assuming a lean FI-CA implementation, Partner Settlement is part of Convergent Invoicing, and it looks very similar to Revenue Distribution. In principle both appear to do the same thing: calculate a share, create credits, and pay out to another party. So the natural reaction is to reuse what already exists.

Can I use these two interchangeably? I will answer straight up. Partner Settlement is not a substitute for Revenue Distribution in every scenario. It depends entirely on what triggers the entitlement. Billing or cash receipt. I will work through why in the rest of the article.

Why does it matter? Because this decision drives the architecture, the scope, and which business functions get activated at the beginning of the project. Those decisions are made early and are usually difficult to reverse later.

 

2. Capability breakdown

Partner Settlement and Revenue Distribution can look interchangeable at a high level, and in several scenarios they produce a similar accounting result. But they are built on different principles, and as shown in the below scenario, the recipient balance does not always end up the same.

 

2.1 How PS-CD Revenue Distribution works

Revenue Distribution is based on cash collection. There are two functional variants of this capability, classic and enhanced. For simplicity, this article focuses only on the classic variant.

Revenue Distribution distributes only amounts that have actually been collected. The collecting authority is effectively holding money on behalf of third parties.

Nothing is distributed until cash is collected. If a payer pays 50% of the receivable, only 50% is available to distribute. The distribution cannot get ahead of the cash, because the cash is what triggers the distribution.

Technically, the distribution run  picks up cleared items and then the posting run creates the credit postings on the final recipient. A clearing reset on the original receivable reverses the previously distributed amount via a debit posting to the final recipient.

 

2.2 How BRIM Partner Settlement works

Partner Settlement addresses a commercial revenue-sharing model. An end user consumes a service, and part of the billed amount belongs to a partner.

The partner's share is determined during billing, not at payment receipt. The customer Billable Item (BIT) is split into a dependent partner item, rated, and posted through Convergent Invoicing to FI-CA as a credit memo. The settlement rule in the partner agreement controls timing and risk behaviour.

Non-payment is handled retrospectively, not prevented upfront. If the partner is configured to bear default risk, when the customer item goes into dunning (the item is open or only partially cleared), then the adjustment run reduces the partner entitlement through a debit adjustment processed in the next settlement run. The sequence is as follows: entitlement first, correction later.

 

2.3 The conceptual difference

The difference is simple:

  • Partner Settlement → entitlement based on billed revenue (accrual)
  • Revenue Distribution → entitlement based on cash collected

Side by side, by characteristics:

Characteristic

PS-CD Revenue Distribution

BRIM Partner Settlement

Business driver

Legislation driven / Collect on behalf of others

Commercial / partner agreement

Entitlement

Cash actually collected

Billed revenue

Trigger

Payment clearing

Customer billing

Partial payment

Distributed as cash clears

Share at billing, adjusted later

Best fit

Cash-based distribution

Commercial revenue sharing

Figure 1  Partner Settlement vs Revenue Distribution – key characteristics

 

3. Test: a real public sector scenario

A local government department collects tax from a taxpayer and forwards it in full to the federal level. The taxpayer owes $100 and pays in three steps:

  • $60 initially, before the due date 
  • $20 after a dunning letter
  • $20 via an external collector

Run that through both capabilities, focusing on the recipient's balance:

Process step

Cash collected (cum.)

Revenue Distribution credits recipient (bal.)

Partner Settlement credits recipient (bal.)

Billing

0

0

100

Payment 1 ($60)

60

60

100

Dunning on open $40

60

60

60 *

Payment 2 ($20)

80

80

60

Payment 3 ($20)

100

100

60

* After the adjustment run reduces the partner entitlement to match the unpaid $40, assuming the partner bears default risk. Without this, the partner balance stays at 100.

Figure 2  Recipient balance through the collection cycle

 

As shown in the table, Revenue Distribution forwards funds as cash arrives and always matches collection.

Partner Settlement credits the full entitlement at billing and subsequently reduces entitlement through the adjustment process (assuming it is configured accordingly). Standard Partner Settlement does not provide a payment-driven mechanism that incrementally rebuilds entitlement as subsequent payments are collected.

In comparison, classic Revenue Distribution never exceeds collected cash. Partner Settlement can temporarily recognise entitlement before cash is received, and additional logic is needed if entitlement must subsequently follow the exact cash collection. In the public sector, where distribution must strictly follow collection, that becomes a design constraint.

 

4. Can Partner Settlement replace Revenue Distribution?

Not entirely and it really depends on the nature of the entitlement.

In my view, Partner Settlement can fit narrow public sector scenarios where payment is effectively received before the share is paid, for example like in prepaid revenue models.

Revenue Distribution remains necessary when entitlement is strictly cash-based, for instance tax sharing, court-ordered collections, or welfare recovery.

 

5. Conclusion

The two capabilities address different business requirements. Partner Settlement follows commercial logic, in terms of what was billed and who owns a share. Revenue Distribution follows cash-based logic and distributes only what has actually been collected.

This distinction is clearly visible the moment payments are delayed, partial, or uncertain, which in public sector scenarios is pretty much business as usual rather than the exception.

The real question is not whether Partner Settlement can produce the same accounting result as Revenue Distribution. The question is whether entitlement starts at billing or only once cash has been collected.

Get the design decision wrong, and the fix later is not a simple configuration change but a different business function or additional custom logic.

 

Disclaimer: This article is for informational purposes only and does not represent official SAP guidance. The examples and approaches are simplified to illustrate possible technical implementation. This is not a complete solution for production use. Do not apply any solution in a production system without fully testing and confirming it in a non-production environment.

entry.title: Re: Custom copy description program between languages
entry.content:
Hello Maria, 

This behavior is covered by standard SAP design. Cost center descriptions are language-dependent and are stored separately per logon language. Therefore, a description maintained in English is not automatically available in Spanish or other languages.

You can refer to https://me.sap.com/notes/0003458548 - How to maintain cost center texts in language dependent

I hope this helps

entry.title: Re: Reallocation cycle in allocating to Overheads line
entry.content:
Hello Ramadevi,

The most likely reason is a difference in allocation/reporting setup or master data between company code 0716 and 071C. GR55 only displays the values based on the report definition, while the reallocation cycle only clears the sender values that are included in the cycle definition. If some cost centers or cost elements are included in the GR55 Overheads line but are not included in the reallocation cycle sender selection for company code 0716, the Overheads line will not become zero.

I hope this helps!

entry.title: SAP Asset Performance Management-Manage Rules and Cumulocity From a Business Perspective
entry.content:
This blog outlines how to trigger an "Alert" and a "Maintenance Notification" within SAP Asset Performance Management(APM) by ingesting time series data via the underlying Embedded IoT platform (Cumulocity). It also covers how to navigate to the Digital Twin , Device Management, and Cockpit of the SAP APM Embedded IoT Platform using "Manage Devices" and  "Map Devices to Technical Objects" applications within SAP APM.

If you want to gain a deep understanding of a few SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) functionalities, check out my SAP APM blog posts links below.

  1. SAP Asset Performance Management Meets SAP Situation Handling - Extended Framework 
  2. SAP Asset Performance Management - Transport Management : Import Transport Requests to Target Tenant 
  3. SAP Asset Performance Management - Ingesting Time Series Data - Derivation & Monitor Rule Management 
  4. SAP Asset Performance Management - Visual Asset Health Monitoring (Part-1) 
  5. SAP Asset Performance Management - Manage Embedded IoT Metadata Sync 
  6. What`s New in SAP Asset Performance Management - Release 2502 
  7. Achieving Sustainability With SAP Asset Performance Management By Leveraging Industrial Systems. 
  8. Configuring Failure Curve Analytics (FCA) In SAP Asset Performance Management (APM)

 

My Business Use Case:

My calcination plant at "Castrop-Rauxel" wants to monitor Vibrations at Drive End for one of the Cooling Water Circulation Pumps, and trigger alerts if the vibration readings deviate from the threshold values. From the business perspective, I have created  three local Indicators in SAP APM representing vibrations at Axial, Horizontal, and Vertical positions for the Cooling Water Circulation Pump Drive End.

Disclaimer : For this demonstration, I have chosen smartphone application capabilities. When we use smartphone wizard for device registration, smartphone is automatically registered by Cumulocity.

Let us first create Cooling Water Circulation Pump-01 (CWCP01) in S/4HANA.

SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) has a two-layered synchronization model:

  1. ERP system to SAP Asset Performance Management (External Synchronization) - This step synchronizes master data, configuration, and transactional data from S/4HANA or SAP ECC into SAP APM.
  2. SAP Asset Performance Management to Embedded IoT (Internal Synchronization) - In this step, technical objects are synchronized from SAP APM to the Embedded IoT layer. This synchronization runs asynchronously as soon as the technical objects and indicators become available in SAP APM.

Once the data is replicated into SAP APM, let us create "Local Indicators" for monitoring Cooling Water Circulation Pump-01 (CWCP01) drive end vibrations at Axial, Horizontal, and Vertical positions.

Cooling Water Circulation Pump-01 Local Indicators

 

Now let us mimic the scenario by connecting the smartphone to "CWCP01". An acceleration sensor is used to detect CWCP01 Drive End Vibrations. A Triaxial Sensor which has three independent internal sensing elements for X, Y, and Z axes.

 

Map Devices To Technical Objects:

Navigate to the Digital Twin Manager by selecting the Map Devices to Technical Objects application from SAP APM. Technical Objects are synchronized from SAP APM to the Embedded IoT platform as Digital Twins.

Map Devices To Technical Objects

Digital Twin is a virtual representation of a physical object or system. It is typically used in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) to help visualize and analyze how a physical object is performing. In the Digital Twin Manager Application, one asset represents one Digital Twin.

In the Digital Twin Manager, we can view all the technical objects and indicators that have been onboarded to SAP Asset Performance Management.

Child technical objects associated with superordinate technical object.

Let us view the Cooling Water Circulation Pump-01 local indicators created in SAP APM under the  "Indicators" tab in the Digital Twin Manager.

Let us link the devices from the Embedded IoT platform with the corresponding Indicators in SAP APM.t us Technical Object Indicators can be linked The "source" identifies the unique ID of the device that is generating the measurement. The status "Linked" means that the indicator is successfully connected to a designated source (managed object/device), and indicates source is fully operational. .

 

 

Mini Note: Ensure that the Unit Of Measure (UoM) of the time series data from the device of the Embedded IoT platform matches with the corresponding SAP APM Indicator UoM.

 

Manage Devices:

The Manage Devices application in the SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) navigates users to Device Management in the Embedded IoT platform. Device Management plays a vital role in building a robust and scalable foundation for IoT solution. It bridges the gap between IoT devices and the business application layer. The devices list contains all devices that are connected to the tenant.

Devices can be grouped according to the business needs. As per my use case, I have created multiple groups to represent Technical Object Type, and assigned devices to groups.

The dash board manager in device management application: 

The measurement tab provides a default visualization of numeric data provided by the device in the form of charts. The screenshot below displays a chart for Cooling Water Circulation Pump Drive End Vibrations with three different series, such as Horizontal, Vertical, and Axial.

 

Since we have linked the device with the SAP APM Indicators, the data flows in and vibration readings for cooling water circulation pump can be viewed in SAP APM Indicator Monitoring Tab.

 

Manage Rules:

To support my business use case, I have set up monitoring rules for the CWCP01 DE sensor in SAP APM. These monitoring rules triggers an alert & notification if the vibration readings deviate from the threshold values.

Monitoring Rules : CWCP01 DE Vibrations (Axial, Horizontal, Vertical)

For example, the rule "CWCP01_DE_AX" condition:

Once the rule executes, If the condition is met, the executed rule triggers an alert and creates a notification. Since the CWCP01 DE Axial vibration reading is > 1mm/s, the executed rule has created an alert and a notification.

 

 

Monitor Alerts:

Now, let us view the triggered alerts in the "Monitor Alerts" application.

 

 

Cockpit:

The cockpit application provides options to manage and monitor IoT assets data from a business perspective, including visualizing data in dashboards and managing reports.

Home Dashboard: This shows the data for the general tenant. It includes active critical alarms, quick links, recent alarms, Data Points, etc. Maintenance team can design the Home Dashboard according to their needs. The dashboard can be customized using "Edit Widgets" option.

Widgets in Dashboard and reports can display maps, images, graphs, tables. Widgets are useful to track information.

Working With Reports:

Reports enable tracking applications, alarms, assets, and other data in a dashboard layout. Reports are global dashboard pages regardless of the asset hierarchy.

In the reports page we can view all the configured reports with their names.

Let us now click on "Cooling Water Circulation Pump/Motor" report.

I have color coded the CWCP01 DE Vibration readings using Edit Widget option.

I have specified the Min. & Max. values for Yellow Range and Red Range.

Now the Data Point Table widget highlights the vibration readings whenever an exception occurs.

Red Range:  Minimum 1.1 mm/s & Maximum 2 mm/s 

Red Range

Yellow Range: Minimum 0.6 mm/s and Maximum 1 mm/s

Yellow Range

 

😊This brings me to the end of the high level walkthrough of SAP Asset Performance Management Embedded IoT features : Digital Twin Manager, Device Management, Cockpit, and Monitoring Rule Management. I hope this blog proves useful to the Plant Maintenance team.

 

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. C

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Digital Compliance Monitoring Cockpit with SAP Risk and Assurance Management
entry.content:
Enterprise Risk and Compliance cockpit image generated by AI

Leading risk and compliance in any organization can sometimes feel like flying a fighter jet straight into a storm with no way of avoiding it. And your mission is to ensure that the plane goes through it without damages and continues its course smoothly.

Daunting, right? But there are upsides: 1. You are not alone since the entire organization – including Senior Management, is onboard, and 2. Contrarily to early wooden planes, yours can benefit from instant data available at your fingertips. Preventing you from finding out that one tank is burst and fuel leaking – slowly but surely – only once the gauge turns to critical red…

 

What makes a good cockpit?

 

When looking at how engineers design plane cockpits, they consider multiple dimensions of course, but with one output in mind: it needs to deliver the right information, at the right time, in the simplest possible way – while enabling immediate action under stressful conditions.

How does that compare to SAP Risk and Assurance Management might you say? Well, let’s continue this analogy!

 

Situational awareness

 

The pilot – here the 2nd line of defense composed of Risk Managers, Compliance Officers, Cybersecurity leads, etc., must understand everything happening around them (compliance coverage, control gaps, cyber threats) instantly.

SAP Risk and Assurance Management helps achieves this by combining data from various systems to show a unified and coherent view of compliance status. Regardless of the compliance initiative: financial governance, IT Security, tax checks, etc.

Controls Overview dashboard app with view of various key metrics of controls

 

Information prioritization

 

At Mach 3, information overload leads to paralysis or worse even, mistakes. The same applies to an organization.

SAP Fiori apps in SAP Risk and Assurance Management have been specifically designed to display only critical data with easy to grasp symbology (icons, colours) to make it obvious what needs to be done.

Manage Risks app

 

Head-Up Display

 

Granted not many Compliance Officers have 3D helmet systems in their office to visualise their compliance progress – but that would be pretty cool, key data is directly in the line of sight with advanced reporting. No need to look at the ground – or Excel sheet – to know at what altitude you are.

Internal control, compliance and risk management information rendered in SAP Analytics Cloud

 

Hands-On Throttle-And-Stick

 

Maybe not as critical in an office than in a plane, but still, the office version of the stick controller is the… mighty computer mouse!

Less clicking, more doing. Critical details are available directly on the line records in the dedicated apps. This reduces the need to search for information so enables users to act rapidly and decisively.

My Controls app

 

High level of automation

 

A digital co-pilot, this is precisely what this is. Alleviating the workload so that Compliance and Risk specialists – but also Senior Management when using this information, can turn their attention to tactics and decision-making. As a result, the system must behave autonomously and manage non-critical tasks itself. These can include categorizing and closing false alerts so that users can spend effort on resolving real issues requiring attention and expertise.

Predicted Conclusions for Issue Management and Remediation

 

Integration of all systems

 

To achieve the automation mentioned in the previous paragraph, there is one prerequisite: everything must work as one system, not separate components. Only then can true automation take place. Navigation, communication, and sensors must be fully integrated so that there is no need to manually reconcile different sources.

Simplified diagram of integration points in SAP Risk and Assurance Management

 

Clear and immediate alert

 

As for a pilot, business process owners must react rapidly to threats and danger. Increasing risk level of course but also control deficiency fit well in this category. Early warnings with real time notification categorized by severity provide them with timely information to address the underlying issues. Alerts in the tool of course, but also sent to the beloved email inboxes.

Email notification with direct link to the record

 

Rapid drill-down capability

 

Should an alert be raised because a control has failed, it is critical to seamlessly transition from awareness to action. Users must be able to quickly navigate from an overview to the underlying details of the inconsistency and initiate the appropriate remediation steps without delay. This ability to navigate efficiently from insights to action ensures that risks are addressed promptly, and compliance is maintained in a continuous and controlled manner.

Direct access to the live source system data for root cause resolution

 

Reliability and redundancy

 

For external stakeholders, process checks performed in SAP Risk and Assurance Management might not be perceived as critical. But when they realize that these control checks relate to compliance, then the perception shifts. This information must remain available for auditors and regulators, but also for Senior Management to support their decision making and provide traceability in case of investigation. Here, backups and fail-safe help prevent failures.

In this area, “SAP cloud solutions provide contractually agreed-upon service-level agreements (SLAs) for resilience and recovery. Customers can enhance these baselines SLAs by selecting premium options appropriate to their business requirements and threat models, including high availability and disaster recovery deployment options” (Securing SAP Cloud Environments)

SAP Trust Center landing page

SAP Risk and Assurance Management enables organizations to continuously oversee risks, detect process deficiencies and compliance issues instantly, and act where it matters most.

As for cockpits in fighter jets, in both cases success depends on integrating critical information into a clear, real-time, and actionable control centre.

What about you, how does your organization design its compliance cockpit? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 17 - Schema evolution testing
entry.content:
When thinking about schema evolution and starting to plan changes while still in development, the "drop-create" strategy is common.

The cds deploy command will generate the appropriate SQL/DDL statements to effect schema changes. Most commonly this command is run in the planning stages with an option to allow the to-be-executed SQL/DDL statements to be checked, but not executed.

What is that option?

This is a question from the June Developer Challenge on CAP Knowledge. And don't forget: always submit your answer as a hash, on its own - read the Taking part section of the intro post for more info. At the end of today, this question will be updated with links to further reading on this topic.

entry.title: Re: Confirmation control
entry.content:
Dear @Zlizardo,

Within the BAdI below, only value of limited standard fields and custom fields can be updated.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/8676e556cca85267e10000000a441470.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

Modify Purchase Order Item (MM_PUR_S4_PO_MODIFY_ITEM)

With this BAdI, you can

  • Set or change values of custom fields in purchase orders on item level and make changes to the following fields:

    TAXCODE, TAXCOUNTRY, INCOTERMSCLASSIFICATION, INCOTERMSTRANSFERLOCATION, INCOTERMSLOCATION1, INCOTERMSLOCATION2, SHIPPINGTYPE, PURCHASINGINFORECORDUPDATECODE

    Note

    PURCHASINGINFORECORDUPDATECODE is an optional parameter and the available values are:

    Fixed

    Short Description

     

    No update

    A

    Update with or without plant

    B

    Update with plant (if no plant ban)

    C

    Update without plant (if no plant requirement)

  • Use the optional importing parameter: PURCHASEORDERITEM_OLD. This parameter contains the item data of the previous roundtrip, for example, the data before the last change was done.

  • Raise messages by filling the changing parameter table MESSAGES

entry.title: Re: Settlement error while settling the project to asset
entry.content:
Hello,

To fix this error, ensure the Profit Center on the WBS element matches the Profit Center on the Asset under Construction (AuC).

Find the mismatch: Check the Profit Center assigned to your WBS element. Then, open the Manage Fixed Assets app, find the AuC, and check its Time-Dependent Data tab to see its Profit Center.

Align the master data: In Manage Fixed Assets, edit the AuC. Update its Cost Center or Profit Center to match the WBS element. If the field is locked, create a New Time Interval valid from the start of the current period.

Best Regards,
Owen

entry.title: Re: Seamless Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On with the Joule Work mobile app
entry.content:
Dear @Santiago_Garcia,

This screen is appearing in case there is another active session on the Cloud Identity Services tenant in your browser context.

An expected scenario for this to happen is for example when you have accessed a trusted web app using the Mobile2Web SSO feature and then accessing a enabled web app for Native-Web trust (usually relying on a normal login) when the previous Mobile2Web SSO session is still valid. This occurs as the previous Mobile2Web SSO session would have a lower "scope" and is not allowed to be reused for the web app that is not enabled for Native-Web trust from SAP Build Work Zone.

In case this occurs for a web app that is configured to use Mobile2Web SSO (using Native-Web trust in SAP Cloud Identity Services), this appears to be a (temporary?) issue. The best in this case would be to open a support incident. Alternatively you could reach out to jouleworkmobile@sap.com in order to discuss the next steps for resolving the issue for you.

Kind regards,
Thomas

entry.title: Re: Logon Failed Error Encountered by Multiple Users
entry.content:
Hi,

"...a "Logon Failed" error while performing different tasks in the system."
What do you mean with "the system"?

The screenshot is so small and unclear that it is impossible to tell what the error message at the bottom is. Also it is of a Crystal Report apparently. You are taking about "different tasks" and "various operations". Are all these tasks and operations related to Crystal Reports? We can only know what you tell us. As you are not telling us very much, we can only take a wild guess.
"I have verified that the required permissions related to the folders have been granted." What folders? What required permissions? Why do you think this is relevant to your issue?

From what I can tell from your screenshot, there is no database name visible. Perhaps that means that you are missing a driver on the affected machines, or you are dealing with a driver version mismatch. Has your B1, Crystal Reports or database version been updated recently?

"...multiple users..."
Does the issue occur on multiple machines as well? You said you can open the layout on the server, can the affected users open the layout on the server as well? How do the affected users access "the system"?

Does your report draw data from multiple databases? Are the affected users new or have they been assigned these tasks and operations recently?

Regards,

Johan

entry.title: Tame Your Agents : 10 Design Patterns for Reliable Agentic AI
entry.content:
What Makes AI "Agentic"?

An AI becomes agentic when it moves beyond single-turn question-answering into a loop: perceive, reason, act, observe, repeat. Instead of returning one response, it autonomously decides which tools to call, what order to call them in, when to ask for clarification, and when to stop.

The shift is architectural, not cosmetic. A chatbot is a function. An agent is a runtime.

This distinction matters because the moment you give a model the ability to choose its next action, you've introduced stochastic behavior into your control flow — not as a bug, but as a design choice. The engineering challenge is containing that stochasticity where it belongs and keeping everything around it predictable.

Quick note before diving in: "Agentic" doesn't mean "LLM." The agent concept — perceive, decide, act — goes back decades, and the "brain" can just as easily be a rule engine, a state machine, or a predictive model (AlphaGo's policy network never saw a token). What's new with LLMs is the generality of the brain — it can handle ambiguous, novel instructions instead of just pre-coded rules. That generality is exactly why it introduces the non-determinism this post focuses on. So before reaching for an LLM agent, ask if a simpler, deterministic brain already solves your problem — it might, and it'll be cheaper and more predictable.

A Pragmatic Note: You Won't Use All Ten

Not all ten patterns apply equally to every system. Here's what production systems actually use:

You'll almost certainly use (core patterns):

  • Prompt Chaining — the simplest base case; most "agents" are really fixed pipelines
  • Workflow Orchestration — every system needs deterministic control flow
  • Tool-Use Routing — how your agent picks which capability to invoke
  • Human-in-the-Loop — any system with real consequences needs approval gates

You'll very likely use (common patterns):

  • Plan-then-Execute — especially in enterprise; plan once, execute deterministically
  • Agentic RAG — for grounding in external knowledge or documents
  • ReAct — reason-act loops happen naturally; you might not name it as such

You'll probably skip or add later (specialized patterns):

  • Memory-Augmented Agents — only for long-running or multi-session agents
  • Reflection/Self-Correction — mainly for code gen and writing tasks
  • Multi-Agent Orchestration — for complex reasoning; most systems are single-agent

The rule of thumb: Start with the core patterns. Add the common patterns that match your problem. Treat the specialized ones as nice-to-haves.

This section maps all ten so you can understand what exists. But don't feel obligated to architect around patterns your system doesn't need.

One critical prerequisite: Data readiness. Industry analyses consistently find that the large majority of AI agent pilots never reach production — and data readiness is the most-cited reason. Agents need structured, trusted, contextualized data. Governance and architecture patterns alone won't save a pilot with siloed, inconsistent, or ungoverned data.

The Core Design Patterns

1. ReAct (Reasoning + Acting)

[Common Pattern]

The foundational pattern. The agent alternates between reasoning steps (thinking about what to do) and action steps (calling a tool, searching the web, writing code). Each observation feeds back into the next reasoning step.

When to use it: General-purpose agents that need to solve multi-step problems with tool access.

The trap: Without constraints, ReAct loops can wander. The model might reason itself into a corner, retry failed actions, or generate plausible-sounding but incorrect observations. You need a loop budget, exit conditions, and output validation — guard rails that deterministic systems never required, because deterministic systems never wandered.

2. Plan-then-Execute

[Core Pattern]

The agent first generates a complete plan — a sequence of steps — and then executes each step independently. Planning and execution are decoupled, which means the plan can be reviewed, edited, or approved by a human before anything happens.

When to use it: High-stakes workflows where you want human oversight. Think: database migrations, financial transactions, customer communications.

The insight: This pattern reintroduces determinism where it matters most. The plan is a fixed artifact; execution follows it mechanically. The stochastic behavior is quarantined to the planning phase, and a human gate sits between planning and execution.

 

3. Agentic RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Agent Control)

[Common Pattern]

An important distinction: RAG itself is not an agentic pattern. Classic RAG is a retrieval pipeline — a linear, static sequence of query → embed → retrieve top-k chunks → stuff into prompt → generate. It has no decision-making, no loops, no autonomy. It's infrastructure, not agency.

What is an agentic pattern is Agentic RAG — where the LLM takes control of the retrieval workflow. Instead of passively consuming whatever chunks a vector search returns, the agent decides when to retrieve, what to search for, whether the results are sufficient, and whether to re-retrieve with a refined query. The retrieval becomes a control loop: retrieve, reason, evaluate, decide to retrieve again or stop.

The analogy from practice: classic RAG is like running one database query and writing your report from whatever comes back. Agentic RAG is a debug loop — you query, inspect the evidence, notice what's missing, refine the query or check a second source, and repeat until you're confident or you hit a cost budget and escalate.

The maturity curve: Naive RAG plateaus quickly on complex queries — precision drops off as questions require synthesizing across multiple sources. Production systems in 2025-2026 have moved to Adaptive RAG, where a classifier routes each query to the appropriate retrieval strategy — direct answer from parametric knowledge for simple factual questions, single-hop retrieval for document lookups, multi-hop retrieval with query decomposition for complex reasoning. The insight: not every question deserves the same retrieval investment, and the agent should be the one making that call.

4. Human-in-the-Loop (HITL)

[Core Pattern]

The agent escalates to a human when confidence is low, stakes are high, or the task crosses a predefined boundary. This isn't a fallback — it's a first-class design pattern.

The design spectrum: HITL exists on a gradient, not as a binary switch. The levels, in increasing order of agent autonomy: observe-only (agent watches, human acts), recommend-with-approval (agent proposes, human approves), execute-with-logging (agent acts, human audits after the fact), and fully autonomous (agent acts, no human review). Most production systems should operate at level two or three, with level four reserved for low-risk, high-volume tasks.

Why it's a pattern, not a compromise: HITL isn't an admission that the AI isn't good enough. It's a recognition that some decisions carry consequences that demand human judgment — legal liability, ethical ambiguity, irreversible actions. The pattern defines where in the workflow the human gate sits, what information the agent surfaces to support the human's decision, and how the human's input flows back into the agent's state.

 

 

 

5. Workflow Orchestration and State Machines

[Core Pattern]

Not every agentic system needs open-ended reasoning. Many production use cases are better served by a deterministic workflow engine that uses AI at specific nodes. The workflow itself — the sequence of steps, the branching logic, the retry policies — is defined as a state machine or a directed acyclic graph (DAG). The AI model is invoked at specific nodes for specific tasks: classification, summarization, extraction, generation.

When to use it: When the business process is well-understood and the steps are known in advance. Think: insurance claims processing, invoice reconciliation, support ticket triage.

Why this pattern wins in enterprise: It gives you the best of both worlds. The workflow is deterministic, auditable, and testable. The AI nodes handle the tasks that are hard to code with rules — understanding natural language, extracting entities from unstructured documents, generating human-readable summaries. You get the flexibility of AI without surrendering control of the process.

The implementation: Frameworks like LangGraph, Temporal, and even traditional BPM engines (Camunda, SAP Build Process Automation) can serve as the orchestration layer. The key design decision is granularity: how much reasoning do you delegate to the model at each node, and how much do you keep in deterministic code?

 

 

 

 

 

6. Tool-Use Routing

[Core Pattern]

The agent acts primarily as an intelligent dispatcher. It receives a request, selects the right tool (or sequence of tools), formats the inputs, and returns the combined result. The intelligence is in the selection, not in multi-step reasoning.

When to use it: When you have a well-defined set of capabilities (APIs, databases, calculators) and the problem is knowing which one to invoke. Think: a customer support agent routing between order lookup, refund processing, and FAQ retrieval.

Why it works: It minimizes the surface area of stochastic behavior. The tools themselves are deterministic; only the routing decision is probabilistic.

 

 

7. Multi-Agent Orchestration

[Specialized Pattern]

Multiple specialized agents collaborate, each responsible for a domain. A coordinator agent delegates tasks, collects results, and synthesizes a final output. Think of it as microservices, but the services are language models with different system prompts and tool sets.

When to use it: Complex workflows that span domains — a research agent that delegates to a search agent, a summarizer, and a fact-checker.

The risk: Compounding variance. If one agent introduces variability, several agents interacting amplify it across the chain. The math is unforgiving: if each step in a five-step chain is 95% reliable, end-to-end reliability is only about 77% (0.95⁵). At 90% per step, you're down to 59%. You need strong contracts between agents: structured outputs, schema validation, and clear handoff protocols.

 

 

8. Reflection and Self-Correction

[Specialized Pattern]

The agent reviews its own output, critiques it, and iterates. This can be a single model re-reading its work, or a separate "critic" model evaluating the "actor."

When to use it: Code generation, writing, analysis — any task where quality improves with revision. The pattern works because language models are often better at evaluating output than generating it on the first attempt.

The subtlety: Reflection can degrade results if the critic is miscalibrated. A model might "correct" a right answer into a wrong one. The purpose of reflection isn't intelligence — it's risk reduction. Knowing when to stop reflecting is itself a design decision.

 

 

 

 

9. Memory-Augmented Agents

[Specialized Pattern]

The agent maintains persistent memory across interactions — episodic memory (what happened in past conversations), semantic memory (facts about the user or domain), and working memory (context for the current task). Memory transforms an agent from stateless to stateful.

When to use it: Long-running assistants, personalized workflows, any scenario where context accumulates over time.

The design challenge: Memory curation. Not everything should be remembered. Stale facts, contradicted preferences, and irrelevant details create noise. You need eviction policies, conflict resolution, and relevance scoring — all problems that traditional databases solved decades ago, now complicated by the fuzziness of natural language.

 

 

 

10. Prompt Chaining (Sequential Pipeline)

[Core Pattern]

The simplest pattern of all — and the one every major framework lists first. You decompose a task into a fixed sequence of LLM calls, where each step's output feeds the next: draft → critique → revise, or extract → classify → summarize. The defining trait is that you hard-code the sequence in advance; the model doesn't decide the order. Optional validation gates sit between steps to catch failures early before they propagate.

When to use it: Whenever a task has clear, stable sub-steps that always run in the same order. If you can write the steps on a whiteboard before the agent runs, this is your pattern. It's the highest-reliability, lowest-cost option because almost nothing is left to the model's discretion except the content of each step.

Why it's where you start: Though it's last in this list, Prompt Chaining is the first pattern you should reach for. Anthropic, OpenAI, Microsoft, and Google all introduce sequential chaining before anything more autonomous, for the same reason: most "agent" problems are actually fixed pipelines in disguise. Reach for a dynamic planner or a multi-agent swarm only after a simple chain demonstrably falls short. The distinction from Plan-then-Execute is exactly this — there, the model generates the plan; here, you did, at design time.

 

What the Major Players Call These Patterns

You won't find the names above used consistently across the industry. Each major vendor has published its own framework with its own vocabulary — but underneath, they're describing the same handful of control-flow shapes. If you've read Anthropic's "Building Effective Agents," OpenAI's "Practical Guide to Building Agents," Microsoft's Azure orchestration patterns, or Google's ADK docs, this table maps their terms back to the patterns in this post.

This post Anthropic (Building Effective Agents) OpenAI (Agents SDK) Microsoft (Azure / Copilot Studio) Google (ADK / Vertex) LangGraph
Prompt ChainingPrompt Chaining(sequential tool/agent calls)Sequential orchestrationSequential agent / SequencingChain (sequential nodes)
ReActAutonomous agent loopAgent + Runner loopGenerative orchestrationReAct agent (LlmAgent loop)create_react_agent (base loop)
Plan-then-Execute— (closest: Orchestrator-Workers)— (Manager plans, then delegates)Magentic (task-ledger planning)Plan-and-execute / SequencingPlan-and-Execute
Agentic RAGAugmented LLM (retrieval)FileSearchTool / hosted retrievalRAG → actionMulti-agent RAG + Vertex AI SearchRetrieval node + conditional edges
Human-in-the-Loop(noted as guardrail)First-class: approvals & interruptionsFirst-class: HITL gates (observer / reviewer / escalation)Safety settings + approval stepsInterrupts / checkpoints
Workflow OrchestrationWorkflows (the umbrella category)Code-first SDK orchestrationDeterministic workflows (Agent Framework)Workflow agents (Sequential / Parallel / Loop)StateGraph
Tool-Use RoutingRoutingHandoffs (as tools)Handoff orchestrationRouting / dispatchConditional edges / Command routing
Multi-Agent OrchestrationOrchestrator-Workers; ParallelizationManager + Handoffs (decentralized)Sequential, Concurrent, Group Chat, MagenticMulti-agent + A2A protocolSupervisor-Worker / Network
ReflectionEvaluator-Optimizer(guardrail / critic agents)Group Chat / Maker-CheckerCritic loop ("refine until critic approves")Reflection loop
MemoryAugmented LLM (memory)Sessions / stateMemory stores (Foundry, Cosmos DB)Memory Bank / PreloadMemoryToolCheckpointer / store

A few things worth noticing in this table.

The vocabulary fragments most around multi-agent work. What this post calls Multi-Agent Orchestration splinters into five different Microsoft terms alone (Sequential, Concurrent, Group Chat, Handoff, Magentic). That's not Microsoft over-engineering — it reflects that "multiple agents working together" is the area with the most distinct sub-shapes and the most ways to go wrong.

Everyone converged on the same simplicity-first message. Anthropic says add complexity "only when simpler solutions fall short." OpenAI says "maximize a single agent's capabilities first." Microsoft warns against "creating unnecessary coordination complexity." Google's ADK leads with single-agent workflow patterns before multi-agent. Four independent teams, the same conclusion — which is the strongest evidence that the restraint this post argues for isn't one opinion, it's the industry consensus.

Guardrails and HITL are now first-class, not afterthoughts. Both OpenAI and Microsoft elevated human-in-the-loop and guardrails to core primitives rather than optional add-ons. That's a direct response to the runaway-cost and reliability failures from the opening of this post — the platforms are building the brakes into the chassis.

The naming you'll encounter most in code: "Supervisor-Worker" (LangGraph), "Orchestrator-Workers" (Anthropic), "Manager" (OpenAI), and "Magentic" (Microsoft) all refer to roughly the same thing — one coordinating agent delegating to specialists. If you internalize that single equivalence, most vendor documentation becomes much easier to read.

Side note for enterprise developers: If these shapes feel familiar, that's because most of them are. Classic Gang-of-Four and enterprise-integration patterns already solved "how do I structure a multi-step process with swappable components and failure handling." Agentic patterns largely reuse that control flow — the one new ingredient is a probabilistic LLM making decisions an if/else block used to make. The structure is old; the decider is new.

Agentic pattern Closest classic pattern Match
Prompt ChainingPipes & Filters / Template MethodTight
Tool-Use RoutingFront Controller / Dispatcher + StrategyTight
Workflow OrchestrationState pattern + SagaTight
Plan-then-ExecuteCommand (queue of command objects)Medium
Multi-Agent OrchestrationMediator (orchestrator) / Chain of Responsibility (handoffs)Medium
Memory-AugmentedMemento + Repository/DAOMedium
Human-in-the-LoopCircuit Breaker (guarded interrupt)Loose
Agentic RAGRepository + lazy loadingLoose
Reflectionfeedback/retry loop (no clean GoF ancestor)Loose
ReAct— (no real OOP equivalent)None

The takeaway: the orchestration rings of an agentic system are familiar enterprise engineering. What's genuinely new — ReAct, Reflection, the "agentic" decision in Agentic RAG — is exactly where a non-deterministic model replaces hard-coded logic, which is why Part 2's discussion of non-determinism matters even if you've built these control-flow shapes a hundred times before.

Wrapping Up Part 1

The ten patterns above are your toolkit. The discipline is in restraint: start with the core patterns (Prompt Chaining, Tool-Use Routing, Workflow Orchestration, Human-in-the-Loop) — and start with the simplest, a plain prompt chain. Add the common ones that fit your problem (Plan-then-Execute, Agentic RAG, ReAct), and treat the specialized patterns (Multi-Agent, Reflection, Memory) as things you earn your way into — not defaults. Every pattern you add buys capability at the cost of latency, tokens, and new failure modes. The teams whose budgets survive are the ones who add agentic structure only when a simpler design demonstrably falls short.

But knowing which pattern to use only gets you halfway. To deploy with confidence, you need to understand why these systems misbehave in the first place — why the same prompt can yield different answers, why small early errors snowball, and how to evaluate something that won't give you the same output twice.

This is Part 1 of a four-part series. Part 1 (this post) is the builder's playbook: the ten design patterns, when to use each, which to avoid, and the guardrails that keep agents in line. Part 2 — "Why Your Agent Won't Behave" goes under the hood: why LLMs are non-deterministic by nature, how that variability compounds, and the architecture that contains it. Part 3 — "The SAP AI Landscape" maps these patterns onto SAP's agentic stack (Joule, Joule Studio, Skills vs. Agents, AI Core, MCP/A2A). Part 4 — "Hands-On" builds a simple Joule agent end to end.

References

Vendor Agent Frameworks (Pattern Mapping)

  • Anthropic. (2024). Building Effective Agents. anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents (Five workflow patterns: prompt chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, evaluator-optimizer.)
  • OpenAI. (2025). A Practical Guide to Building Agents. cdn.openai.com (Manager and decentralized/handoff orchestration; guardrails as first-class primitives.)
  • Microsoft. (2026). AI Agent Orchestration Patterns. Azure Architecture Center. learn.microsoft.com (Sequential, Concurrent, Group Chat, Handoff, Magentic patterns.)
  • Microsoft. (2026). Multi-agent Orchestration Patterns and Best Practices. Copilot Studio. learn.microsoft.com
  • Google Cloud. (2026). Agent Development Kit (ADK). Vertex AI / Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform. cloud.google.com (Sequential, Parallel, Loop workflow agents; routing; multi-agent + A2A.)
  • LangChain. LangGraph Multi-Agent Patterns. langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph (Supervisor-Worker, Network, ReAct base loop, plan-and-execute, reflection.)

Agentic AI Before LLMs

  • Brooks, R. A. (1986). A Robust Layered Control System for a Mobile Robot. IEEE Journal of Robotics and Automation, 2(1), 14-23. (Subsumption architecture — purely reactive, rule-based agents, pre-dating LLMs.)
  • Silver, D., Huang, A., Maddison, C. J., et al. (2016). Mastering the Game of Go with Deep Neural Networks and Tree Search. Nature, 529, 484-489. (AlphaGo: agentic decision-making via policy/value networks — zero language modeling.)

10. Prompt Chaining (Sequential Pipeline)

  • Anthropic. (2024). Building Effective Agents — Prompt Chaining pattern. anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents
  • Wu, T., Terry, M., & Cai, C. J. (2022). AI Chains: Transparent and Controllable Human-AI Interaction by Chaining Large Language Model Prompts. CHI 2022. arXiv:2110.01691 (Foundational work on decomposing tasks into chained LLM calls with intermediate checkpoints.)

1. ReAct (Reasoning + Acting)

  • Yao, S., Zhao, J., Yu, D., Du, N., Shafran, I., Narasimhan, K., & Cao, Y. (2023). ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models. 11th International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2023). arXiv:2210.03629

2. Plan-then-Execute

  • Xu, B., Peng, Z., Lei, B., Mukherjee, S., Liu, Y., & Xu, D. (2023). ReWOO: Decoupling Reasoning from Observations for Efficient Augmented Language Models. arXiv:2305.18323
  • Wang, L., Xu, W., Lan, Y., Hu, Z., Lan, Y., Lee, R. K.-W., & Lim, E.-P. (2023). Plan-and-Solve Prompting: Improving Zero-Shot Chain-of-Thought Reasoning by Large Language Models. Proceedings of the 61st Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2023). arXiv:2305.04091
  • Huang, X., Liu, W., Chen, X., et al. (2024). Understanding the Planning of LLM Agents: A Survey. arXiv:2402.02716

3. Agentic RAG

  • Lewis, P., Perez, E., Piktus, A., Petroni, F., Karpukhin, V., Goyal, N., Küttler, H., Lewis, M., Yih, W., Rocktäschel, T., Riedel, S., & Kiela, D. (2020). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2020). arXiv:2005.11401
  • Singh, A., Ehtesham, A., Kumar, S., Khoei, T. T., & Vasilakos, A. V. (2025). Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Survey on Agentic RAG. arXiv:2501.09136
  • Asai, A., Wu, Z., Wang, Y., Sil, A., & Hajishirzi, H. (2024). Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection. ICLR 2024. arXiv:2310.11511

4. Human-in-the-Loop (HITL)

  • Wu, Q., Bansal, G., Zhang, J., Wu, Y., Li, B., Zhu, E., Jiang, L., et al. (2023). AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation. First Conference on Language Modeling (COLM 2024). arXiv:2308.08155 (Sections on human-in-the-loop agent patterns and UserProxyAgent design.)
  • Xu, B., et al. (2025). Plan-Then-Execute: An Empirical Study of User Trust and Team Performance When Using LLM Agents As A Daily Assistant. arXiv:2502.01390
  • LangGraph Documentation. Human-in-the-Loop Patterns. docs.langchain.com

5. Workflow Orchestration and State Machines

  • LangGraph (LangChain). Stateful Orchestration with Checkpointing. github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph
  • Fourney, A., Bansal, G., Mozannar, H., et al. (2024). Magentic-One: A Generalist Multi-Agent System for Solving Complex Tasks. arXiv:2411.04468 (Orchestration architecture with ledger-based control flow.)
  • Shen, Y., Song, K., Tan, X., Li, D., Lu, W., & Zhuang, Y. (2023). HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face. NeurIPS 2023. arXiv:2303.17580 (Task decomposition and deterministic orchestration via LLM controller.)

6. Tool-Use Routing

  • Schick, T., Dwivedi-Yu, J., Dessì, R., Raileanu, R., Lomeli, M., Zettlemoyer, L., Cancedda, N., & Scialom, T. (2023). Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools. NeurIPS 2023. arXiv:2302.04761
  • Patil, S. G., Zhang, T., Wang, X., & Gonzalez, J. E. (2023). Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs. NeurIPS 2024. arXiv:2305.15334
  • Qin, Y., Liang, S., Ye, Y., Zhu, K., Yan, L., et al. (2023). ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-World APIs. ICLR 2024. arXiv:2307.16789

7. Multi-Agent Orchestration

  • Wu, Q., Bansal, G., Zhang, J., et al. (2023). AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation. First Conference on Language Modeling (COLM 2024). arXiv:2308.08155
  • Guo, T., Chen, X., Wang, Y., Chang, R., Pei, S., Chawla, N. V., Wiest, O., & Zhang, X. (2024). Large Language Model Based Multi-Agents: A Survey of Progress and Challenges. IJCAI 2024. arXiv:2402.01680
  • Hong, S., Zhuge, M., Chen, J., et al. (2023). MetaGPT: Meta Programming for A Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework. ICLR 2024. arXiv:2308.00352
  • Qian, C., Liu, W., Liu, H., et al. (2023). ChatDev: Communicative Agents for Software Development. ACL 2024. arXiv:2307.07924

8. Reflection and Self-Correction

  • Shinn, N., Cassano, F., Gopinath, A., Narasimhan, K., & Yao, S. (2023). Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning. NeurIPS 2023. arXiv:2303.11366
  • Madaan, A., Tandon, N., Gupta, P., Hallinan, S., Gao, L., Wiegreffe, S., et al. (2023). Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback. NeurIPS 2023. arXiv:2303.17651
  • Pan, L., Saxon, M., Xu, W., Nathani, D., Wang, X., & Wang, W. Y. (2023). Automatically Correcting Large Language Models: Surveying the Landscape of Diverse Self-Correction Strategies. arXiv:2308.03188

9. Memory-Augmented Agents

  • Park, J. S., O'Brien, J. C., Cai, C. J., Morris, M. R., Liang, P., & Bernstein, M. S. (2023). Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior. 36th Annual ACM Symposium on User Interface Software and Technology (UIST 2023). arXiv:2304.03442
  • Packer, C., Wooders, S., Lin, K., Fang, V., Patil, S. G., Stoica, I., & Gonzalez, J. E. (2023). MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems. arXiv:2310.08560
  • Wang, G., Xie, Y., Jiang, Y., Mandlekar, A., Xiao, C., Zhu, Y., Fan, L., & Anandkumar, A. (2023). Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models. NeurIPS 2023. arXiv:2305.16291
  • Zhang, Z., et al. (2024). A Survey on the Memory Mechanism of Large Language Model based Agents. arXiv:2404.13501

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 17 - Schema evolution testing
Issue with custom color theme after kernel update
entry.content:
After update to kernel version 7.93 UNICODE Patch 315, we have received some incidents with custom Themes on Fiori, most of which comes from color changes in text box background of 1 in 4 from normal transparent to fixed color #eff1f2 in our custom theme following these customizing theme (OLEON White, Blue, Red, Green), and only White one is the one got bugged.

After some looking up on sap portal, we found some SAP notes but not anything quite likely the one we are facing. Could you help us know what happens, is this may cause by kernel itself or the coding of CSS files is misconfigure.This is the one got buggedThis is normal and others theme

entry.title: After upgrading to S/4HANA, a Z-field in the purchase order header gets cleared in the database.
entry.content:
Hello experts,

We recently upgraded from ECC to S/4HANA in RISE. Following the upgrade, we encounter the following issue: when running ME22N, changing a standard field value in the purchase order header, and clicking SAVE, a custom Z-field located in the Customer Data tab (header level) gets cleared (reset to initial) in the database.

The system implements the BAdI ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST.

In the method IF_EX_ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST~FIELDSELECTION_HEADER, all fields are defined as modifiable except for this Z-field:

method IF_EX_ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST~FIELDSELECTION_HEADER.

FIELD-SYMBOLS: <fs> LIKE LINE OF ch_fieldselection.

READ TABLE ch_fieldselection ASSIGNING <fs> WITH TABLE KEY metafield = mmmfd_cust_01.

IF sy-subrc IS INITIAL. "10

<fs>-fieldstatus = '.'. " Input

ENDIF.

Even if we comment out the IF statement on line 10 above—making the Z-field modifiable as well—the Z-field still gets cleared (and a message is received stating that the field cannot be changed).

Originally, this Z-field was updated inside the method IF_EX_ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST~PROCESS_HEADER. However, even after deleting the update code in method process_header, the field still resets in the database.

METHOD if_ex_me_process_po_cust~process_header.

DATA: ls_head TYPE mepoheader,

lv_zolla TYPE lfm1-zolla,

ls_lfa1 TYPE lfa1.

INCLUDE mm_messages_mac.

ls_head = im_header->get_data( ).

. . .

DATA: lv_zzfld TYPE zebeln

,read_eblen_from_ekko_sy_subrc TYPE sy-subrc

,ls_headx TYPE mepoheaderx

,lo_header_ref2cl TYPE REF TO cl_po_header_handle_mm .

 

SELECT SINGLE zzfld FROM ekko INTO lv_zzfld WHERE ebeln = ls_head-ebeln.

read_eblen_from_ekko_sy_subrc = sy-subrc.

 

DATA: lt_callstack_1 TYPE abap_callstack

,lt_callstack_2 TYPE sys_callst.

CALL FUNCTION 'SYSTEM_CALLSTACK'

EXPORTING

max_level = 0

IMPORTING

callstack = lt_callstack_1

et_callstack = lt_callstack_2.

 

READ TABLE lt_callstack_1 INTO DATA(ls_callstack_1) WITH KEY blockname = 'BAPI_PO_CHANGE'.

IF sy-subrc NE 0. "IF the code is not called from BAPI_PO_CHANGE

IF me->m_zzfld_old_val <> lv_zzfld.

IF read_eblen_from_ekko_sy_subrc EQ 0 AND lv_zzfld IS NOT INITIAL.

ls_head-zzfld = lv_zzfld.

lo_header_ref2cl ?= im_header.

lo_header_ref2cl->get_datax( IMPORTING ex_data = ls_headx ).

me->m_zzfld_old_val = ls_head-zzfld.

lo_header_ref2cl->my_ibs_firewall_on = 'X'.

lo_header_ref2cl->my_cust_firewall_on = 'X'.

ls_headx-zzfld = 'X'.

lo_header_ref2cl->set_datax( im_data = ls_headx ).

CALL METHOD im_header->set_data EXPORTING im_data = ls_head.

im_header->set_changed( ). "update the changes also in DB

ENDIF.

ENDIF.

ENDIF.

 

I tried moving the above update code to the POST method, but that did not resolve the issue either.

The only solution that worked was creating a Function Module (FM) that performs a direct update on the Z-field and calling it from the IF_EX_ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST~POST method.

Is this a good solution? Isn't it risky to write a direct UPDATE statement to a standard SAP table?

FM => ZUPD_ZZ_FLD:

  1. In the Attributes tab, under Processing Type, select: Update Module
  2. Underneath, select: Start Immediately
  3. In the Import tab, define two parameters (ensuring Pass Value is checked for both):
    • IM_EBELN TYPE EBELN
    • IM_ZZ_FLD TYPE [Your Field Type]
  4. In the Source Code tab, write the following code:

FUNCTION ZUPD_ZZ_FLD.

UPDATE ekko SET zz_fld = im_zz_fld WHERE ebeln = im_ebeln.

ENDFUNCTION.

 

METHOD if_ex_me_process_po_cust~post.

DATA: ls_mepoheader TYPE mepoheader.

me->m_zz_fld_old_val = 450001234 .

IF m_zz_fld_old_val IS NOT INITIAL AND im_header IS BOUND.

ls_mepoheader = im_header->get_data( ).

IF ls_mepoheader-ebeln IS NOT INITIAL.

CALL FUNCTION 'ZUPD_ZZ_FLD' IN UPDATE TASK

EXPORTING

im_ebeln = ls_mepoheader-ebeln

im_zz_fld = m_zz_fld_old_val.

CLEAR m_zz_fld_old_val.

ENDIF.

ENDIF.

 

Thankyou,

Hagit

entry.title: Re: Blog 3 - From BW 7.5 to SAP BDC: Reusing Extractors, Custom Logic, and Models Standard Extracto
entry.content:
Hello @torsten_wirth 

regarding the key fields... usually every BW / BW4 content datasource has documented key fields within the target DSOs in the SAP help pages. When you implemented those in the past, you also used the content DSO as template or checked the documentation, right? I think this is no difference to the old days 😉

Regarding the native approach... I think it depends. As of now, not all 2LIS extractors can be substituted by CDS Views, e.g. for 2LIS_03_UM I have not yet found the appropriate CDS view.

For others, you are definitely right, and I would prefer CDS Views over 2LIS, whenever possible.

BR, Martin

entry.title: Contract -> Customer Order -> Production Order PP04
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

does anyone have experience with this topic in standard SAP?

We are working with quantity contracts. Based on these, a sales order is created, which in turn automatically triggers a production order (assembly processing / make-to-order scenario).

In the related production order (General tab / Sales order field), the system always shows the quantity contract instead of the derived sales order.

Is there any configuration option to ensure that the sales order is referenced in the production order instead of the quantity contract? I have not found any indication in customizing so far. Currently, it seems to be standard SAP behavior that the quantity contract is always referenced in the production order rather than the derived sales order.

Is anyone familiar with this behavior or able to provide further details?

Thanks, BG Thorsten

SAP R/3 MAN Production Planning (PP)

entry.title: The June Edition of SAP Oil, Gas, and Energy Newsletter Is Out
entry.content:
This edition follows SAPPHIRE 2026, where SAP announced the next step in its vision for the Autonomous Enterprise. If you missed the news or want to go deeper on what was announced, we've pulled together the key highlights relevant to oil, gas, and energy — and you'll find the link inside.

The big theme running through this edition is AI — specifically, why so many energy companies nail the pilot and stall on everything after. We dig into what actually separates the companies that transform from the ones that don't. If you're somewhere in that gap right now, this edition is worth your time.

Sneak Peek:

Harbour Energy: From Two Years to One Month

Instead of forcing a single ERP across a sprawling post-acquisition portfolio, Harbour Energy connected their systems intelligently — cutting integration time from two years to one month. It's one of the more honest and practical transformation stories we've run.

AI and the Autonomous Enterprise — But Foundation First

Your AI proof of concept showed promise. So what comes next? Most energy leaders underestimate the distance between pilot and full implementation. We also cover ANYbotics and SAP, who are deploying autonomous robots for industrial inspections in environments too hazardous for human access — and routing that data directly into business systems. Not a concept. It's live.

Carbon Capture and Storage: Moving Faster Than Expected

CCS is no longer a talking point. Oil, gas, and energy companies are building real commercial infrastructure around "Transport and Store as a Service" — and the integrated digital foundation you put in place today will determine how quickly you can move on it.

More from This Edition

• Siemens Energy — overhauled financial consolidation using SAP Cloud ERP and SAP Analytics Cloud, enabling more agile business steering across group and local levels.
• Messer Americas — the world's largest privately held gases specialist, on achieving data-driven precision in a fast-moving market.
• NextDecade — accelerating digital adoption at scale with AI-assisted workflows.
• GPA Add-On for SAP PRA — a new facility-level accounting layer for complex gas processing, commingled streams, and multiple conversion points. No custom coding required.
• The Digital Backbone — our new podcast series kicks off with a candid conversation on why master data problems are so easy to ignore until they aren't.

On the Calendar

June 24, 2026 — Webinar: Building the Autonomous Enterprise in Oil, Gas and Energy (online)
July 30, 2026 — Oklahoma City Innovation Day, Oklahoma City, US
September 2, 2026 — Houston Innovation Day, Houston, Texas, US
October 27–29, 2026 — SAP for Asset Management Conference, Seville, Spain

FYI

We publish every two months. If a colleague shared this with you and you'd like it in your inbox directly, use the sign-up link in the newsletter or reach out to us at SAPOilandGasNews@sap.com.

Be active on the SAP Oil, Gas, and Energy Community Group - a space to ask questions, exchange knowledge, and stay connected between editions.

See you in August.

— The SAP Oil, Gas, and Energy Newsletter Team
